topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, a jarring intrusion on a perfectly mundane Tuesday afternoon. A profile picture flashed onto my screen—a grainy, overly-filtered shot of someone I hadn't seen in fifteen years, grinning with that same irritating smirk. My jaw tightened instantly. It was Mark. Mark, the architect of my early awkwardness, the one whose shadow I’d spent years trying to outrun. A slow, cold burn started low in my stomach. I didn't even need to click on it. I just stared at the little blue button, my thumb hovering over it like a coiled spring. *What now?* I thought, a sharp, almost brittle laugh catching in my throat. I deleted the request before I could even process the impulse, the silence in my room suddenly thick with contained energy.

[story 2]

I picked up my phone, the glass cool against my palm. The name ‘Mark R.’ swam up on the screen, and a wave of pure annoyance washed over me, quickly hardening into something sharper. I remembered the locker slams, the whispered taunts in the hallway, the way his presence always seemed to suck the air out of a room. Seeing his name pop up now, a casual, unsolicited invitation into my digital space, felt like a deliberate provocation. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms so tightly my knuckles turned white. I didn't respond. Instead, I scrolled through my contacts, deliberately ignoring the little prompt. A flicker of something defiant sparked behind my eyes—a refusal to let him dictate the temperature of my mood.

[story 3]

The chime sounded, demanding attention. It was a connection request from Mark. I felt a familiar tightening in my chest, a quickening pulse that wasn't entirely fear, but something far more insistent. I looked at the profile picture—the same smug expression that used to make my skin crawl. A slow, deliberate breath escaped me. I didn't immediately swipe it away. Instead, I let my gaze linger for a beat too long, a silent challenge passing between me and the screen. My lips curved into a slight, unimpressed downturn. I typed out a single, curt message, something sharp and dismissive, before immediately locking my phone and turning my back on it, the action radiating a clear sense of unbothered dismissal.
